steps.title: Tie processing types to capital needs
steps.content: The assertion says firms initially had limited capital and that this barrier improved in the 2000s. Processing imports with assembly does not require paying for materials, while processing with inputs does. A sharp rise in inputs best supports greater capital availability.

steps.title: Use the data trend from 2000 to 2006
steps.content: The graph pattern says processing imports with inputs increased far faster than processing with assembly over 2000 to 2006. That specific rise tracks exactly the claim about firms becoming less constrained by limited capital.

steps.title: Reject comparisons that do not show growth in inputs
steps.content: Choice A makes a 2006 ranking claim and conflicts with ordinary imports being higher that year. Choice C uses a static comparison and misses the needed change over time. Choice D compares categories across different years without supporting capital growth. Choice B directly captures a stronger rise in the capital-intensive category. The answer is B.
